coredata.dc:title: Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs biocompatible nano-carriers for smart and controlled targeted drug delivery with pH-triggered and ultrasound irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Hafezi, Media
Rostami, Mojtaba
Hosseini, Asieh
Rahimi-Nasrabadi, Mehdi
Fasihi-Ramandi, Mahdi
Badiei, Alireza
Ahmadi, Farhad
coredata.dc:description: In the present study a pH/ultrasound (US)-responsive nano-carriers based on magnetic core-shell ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs ternary composite was prepared. The smart nano-carrier contain a magnetic zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4) core, mesoporous zinc oxide (mZnO) and graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (N-GQDs) interlayer shell that prepared by sol-gel and/or the hydrothermal methods. This nano-carrier was used for pH/ultrasound-controllable curcumin (Cur) reagent release. The structural, thermal and morphological properties of each nano-carrier was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), surface area measurements, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAEDP) image, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), thermo gravimetric analyzer (TGA), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET). Also, the cytotoxicity assay of nano-carriers have been studied on the fibroblast and Hela cells. No in vitro cytotoxic effects have observed at high concentration (75 μg/mL) of nanocarriers. The Cur drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers were as high as 76.45% w/w (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs) and 64.71% w/w (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs). The loaded Cur can be sustainably released, and could be controlled by US irradiation and pH adjustment. Thus, these core-interlayer-shells are suitable for smart and controlled targeted drug release by using pH and US irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smart nano-carrier
Drug delivery
pH-triggered
Us-irradiation
Controlled release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@25 wt% N-GQDs or ZnFe2O4@ mZnO@5 wt% GQDs
In vitro drug loading of nanocarriers
In vitro release behavior of Nano-carriers study
In vitro anti-cancer activity and therapeutic efficacy study on the HeLa cell line
Cytotoxicity study of nano-carriers against normal human fibroblast cells
Drug loading and controlled releaseing of nanocarriers

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Institute for Medical Research Development

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of pure ZnFe2O4, ZnO NPs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nanocarriers.
(a, b, c, d, e and f). SEM images of the N-GQDs (a); ZnFe2O4@mZnO (b) and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs (c); EDX analysis for the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs (d); TEM (e) and SAED (f) images of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs.
(a, b, c and d). FT-IR spectra of the (a) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs-Cur, (b) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs, (c) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs-Cur and (d) Cur powder.
Magnetic hysteresis loops of the ternary composite nanocarriers.
TGA–DTA curves of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and N-GQDs.
(a, b, c, d, e and f). (a, b, c, d) Zeta potential measurement of nano-carriersat different pH (5.5 and 7.4) and (e and f) size distribution of nanocarriers.
(a, and b). BET adsorption–desorption isotherms (a) and the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curve (b) of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs.

(a and b). Effect of ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- QDs -Cur, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs -Cur, and Cur on the cell viability after incubation for 24 (a) and 48 h (b) at different doses.
(a and b). Cell viability (MTT assay) of primary normal human fibroblast cells, IC50, against various gradient concentrations of nanocarriers after 24 (a) and 48 (b) hours of exposure.

(a and b). Cumulative drug release curves of Cur-loaded nano-carriersat 37 °C. (a) For ultrasound and pH dually responsive nanocarriers; (b) for solely pH-responsive. The Cur release experiments were carried out at different times (t = 3, 5, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h) with 15 min ultrasound radiation and different pH values (7.4 and 5.5).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of N-GQDs.

Graphical representation showing the preparation process of the multifunctional ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs core-interlayer-shell nanostructures and the subsequent Cur loading and smart release control with pH and Us-irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the electrostatic interactions between of nano-carrierssurface and cancer cell membrane.
Schematic illustration of the interactions between of N-GQDs and Cur.
Coercivity, remanence, and saturation magnetization of the products.
SBET, Pvol, and Psize of the synthesized ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs.
The percentage of drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers after 24 h.
Cumulative drug release profiles of Cur-loaded nano-carriersat 37 °C in pH 5.5 and 7.4 buffer after 96 h, for ultrasound and pH dually responsive nanocarriers.
Cumulative drug release profiles of Cur-loaded nanocarriers at 37 °C in pH 5.5 and 7.4 buffer after 24 h, for pH responsive nanocarriers.

head.ce:title: Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs biocompatible nano-carriers for smart and controlled targeted drug delivery with pH-triggered and ultrasound irradiation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy-International Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study a pH/ultrasound (US)-responsive nano-carriers based on magnetic core-shell ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs ternary composite was prepared. The smart nano-carrier contain a magnetic zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4) core, mesoporous zinc oxide (mZnO) and graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (N-GQDs) interlayer shell that prepared by sol-gel and/or the hydrothermal methods. This nano-carrier was used for pH/ultrasound-controllable curcumin (Cur) reagent release. The structural, thermal and morphological properties of each nano-carrier was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), surface area measurements, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAEDP) image, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), thermo gravimetric analyzer (TGA), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET). Also, the cytotoxicity assay of nano-carriers have been studied on the fibroblast and Hela cells. No in vitro cytotoxic effects have observed at high concentration (75 μg/mL) of nanocarriers. The Cur drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers were as high as 76.45% w/w (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs) and 64.71% w/w (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs). The loaded Cur can be sustainably released, and could be controlled by US irradiation and pH adjustment. Thus, these core-interlayer-shells are suitable for smart and controlled targeted drug release by using pH and US irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most serious oldest diseases is cancer that is the sum of multiple genetic mutations diseases which happened by corporeal DNA replication error, genetic defects, environmental issues and harmful foods. The third leading cause of death in the world is cancer and annually costs the billions of dollars. The most methods that are used to treat various cancers are surgery, phototherapy and chemotherapy. Chemotherapy is the most common of these, but the use of chemotherapeutic drugs are concentration dependent and are toxic for other tissues. To dissolve of these longstanding challenges the nanotechnology has provide a lot of facilities especially in development of smart nano-carrier for selective delivering of drugs [1].

In recent decades, the emergence of magnetic smart nano-carriers for tumor diagnosis and treatment has brought a new dawn to cancer treatment. These smart nano-carriers like zinc ferrite nano-particles can carry an anti-cancer drug like Cur and directed the drug to the tumor cell site by superparamagnetic field and therefore, achieve the purpose of treatment of cancer patients [2–5].

The last years have witnessed the development of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). N-GQDs is a kind of zero-dimensional (0D) graphene (2D) nano-sheets with lateral nano-sizes less than 100 nm [6]. In fact the N-GQDs or GQDs have the properties of both G and quantum dots (QDs) and considered as a natural candidate superior to traditional semiconductor QDs because of their exhibited unique photoluminescence properties, optical and electronic characteristics, biocompatibility, excellent stability, high aqueous/lipophilic solubility, high resistance to photobleaching, and furthermore environmental and friendly precursors [7–10]. These outstanding properties make N-GQDs or GQDs potentially applicable in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting nano-materials, bioluminescent materials, optoelectronic functional nano-composites, drug and gene delivery, bioimaging, chemosensor, biosensors and photovoltaic devices [11–16]. The N-GQDs prepared by various methods such as: laser ablation, microwave assisted pyrolysis, chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal decomposition process, electro-chemical oxidation [17–22]. The recent publications reports the N-GQDs nano-hybrids with metal oxides, carbon nano-structures [23,24]. With the development in nano-biotechnology, interest in N-GQDs-based nano-hybrids has rapidly grown and they have shown great potential as important candidates in imaging, photothermal therapy, antibacterial activity, drug and gene delivery, and biosensor development [10,25–29]. N-GQDs-based nano-materials for biomedical applications should be nontoxic, high water-dispersible, and biocompatible [30].

The applied of magnetic core-shell ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs ternary composite nanocarriers to improve of US absorption and magnetic properties can be considered as a hotspot study in various medical fields. Due to non-toxic and biodegradable properties of ZnFe2O4@mZnO NPs, they are considered as a potent candidate for drug carriers. ZnO slowly dissolved in acidic tumor cells media, therefore, considered as pH sensitive carriers (shown in Scheme 2) [31]. In addition, the surface of ZnO able to produce active oxide, hydrogen peroxide and superoxide and therefore, considered as a strong anticancer agent [32]. Also a significant attention on semiconductor ZnFe2O4@mZnO nanomaterial as anticancer drug carriers were growing and still need to be studied more. Ultrasonic waves are suitable for the controlled release of the anti-cancer drug curcumin because these waves have a greater depth of penetration into the body compared to other radiological methods. In addition, due to the precise control of US radiation by power and time of use, drug release can be performed in a completely controlled manner [33–36]. It is well known that the pH of tumors tissues are relatively low, so this make an opportunity to make a pH-sensitive nano materials for selective carry and release of the drug [37–39]. So, preparation of new nano compound carriers which have both responsibility to US and pH for dual controls of drugs, has become one of the newest areas of research.

The core-interlayer-shell is one type of these nano compounds, which contains mesoporous ZnO shells that increase the amount of loading capacity and provide a better combination of ZnFe2O4@mZnO with N-GQDs. On the other hand, it is a hybrid nano-composite that is compatible with the tissue target and also controlled release of drug by pH and/or US. Herein, we report a multi-step procedure for preparing multi-functional core-interlayer-shell nano-structures consist of ZnO pellets coated with ZnFe2O4 core and N-GQDs interlayer of ZnFe2O4@mZnO, then modified with N-GQDs to increase the stability of the ZnFe2O4@mZnO nano-structures against the reticuloendothelial system (RES) [40]. N-GQDs coating can improve the hydrophilicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility, increase stability and blood circulation of the as-synthesized nano-structures. This work is the first report of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs as smart nano-carriers to deliver Cur and provide its controlled release in a pH/US-responsive manner. This multi-functional nano-structures could be used as smart biodegradable nano-carrier for medical goals and controlled targeted drug delivery using pH and US irradiation.

In recent years, cur has been given a lot of attention due to its unique properties such as chemopreventive or anti-cancer activities with minimal side effects [41,42]. Especially the growth inhibiting characteristics of cur has been reported against many tumor cell lines, including cancers of the cervical, colon, bladder, prostate and breast. However, the low solubility of curcumin in water and consequently its poor absorption after oral administration has limited its clinical use [43]. Coating the surface of ZnFe2O4@mZnO nanocarriers with hydrophilic N-GQDs increases its hydrophilicity and thus improve its effect on cancer cells. So, we prepared a hydrophilic nanocarriers ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs-Cur from the insoluble commercial Cur and poorly soluble ZnO nano-structures, consequently enhancing its biological importance.

Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (Mw = 297.49 g/mol), FeCl3·6H2O (Mw = 270.43 g/mol), ethanol, urea, citric acid monohydrate (CA, C6H8O7.H2O, M w = 208 g/mol), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SD, NaC12H25SO4, Mw = 288.372 g/mol) and cetyl trimethyl-ammonium bromide (CTAB, C19H42BrN, Mw = 364.45 g/mol) were prepared from Merck (Berlin, Germany). The pure and deionized water (DI) was obtained by an ultra-pure water system (Smart-2-Pure, TKACo, Berlin, Germany). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT), phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. The other reagents were prepared from Merck.

The sol-gel procedure was used to prepare the ZnFe2O4 nano-particles (NPs) [44]. The resulted ZnFe2O4 NPs was coated with mesoporous ZnO shell by sol-gel method as follows: 1 g of ZnFe2O4 NPs were dispersed by ultrasonication for 15 min in a solution contains 100 mL DI + 200 mL ethanol containing citric acid:CTAB (1:2) solution. Then, 300 mL of solution contain 2.5 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was added drop wise to the ZnFe2O4 NPs dispersion under mechanical stirring. The solution stirred for 3 h at room temperature to complete the reaction and obtained a homogeneous solution, then the product was dried at 110 °C for 24 h. Finally the resulting ZnFe2O4@mZnO was sintered in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h (shown in Scheme 2).

The N-GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal pyrolysis process. Briefly, 2 g CA was dissolved into 30 mL DI + 0.5 g urea and stirred to form a transparent solution. Then the mixture was put into a 40 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 5 h to melt and pyrolyze. Finally the dark brown coloured N-GQDs solution was obtained (shown in Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@25 wt% N-GQDs or ZnFe2O4@ mZnO@5 wt% GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: ZnFe2O4@mZnO (1 g) was dispersed into 25 wt% of N-GQDs or GQDs aqueous brown solution and kept at room temperature under mechanical stirring for 24 h till it became a homogeneous mixture. Then the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@25 wt% N-GQDs or ZnFe2O4@ mZnO@25 wt% GQDs was collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The final product was dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 12 h (shown in Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug loading of nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para:
The Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nano-carriers was resulted by precipitation method using the acetone as solvent. Firstly, 1 mg of Cur was dissolved in 10 mL of acetone and then slowly added into 50 mL of DI water containing 60 mg of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs or ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nano-carriers under stirring condition at room temperature. The mixing continued until the acetone completely evaporated and drug loaded nano-carriers were prepared. The final product was separated by centrifuging at 7000 rpm for 20 min, then washed with DI water repeatedly and the obtained nano-carriers were dried at ambient atmosphere overnight. Also the amount of residual Cur in supernatant was measured spectrophotometricalty at wavelength of 420 nm. The Cur loading efficiency (LE %) was calculated using the following equation:
LE
%
=
total
Cur − supernatant free amount of
Cur
total
Cur
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release behavior of Nano-carriers study
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs or ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nano-carrier was transferred into a beaker containing certain amount of release media and continuously measured the released Cur as follows: 6 mg of Cur-loaded nano-carrier was dispersed in 50 mL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (0.01 M; pH 7.4 or pH 5.5) in a beaker (100 mL) at 37 °C. Then, 4 mL of the above mixture solution in the times of 3, 5, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h, was drawn intermittently and transferred into the middle of ultrasonic cleaner bath, to exposed on ultrasound irradiation with power of 100 W for 15 min. Then, the solution were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to separate the particles from medium. The absorbance of suspension was measured at 420 nm and the concentration of Cur was obtained from standard calibration curve. Cumulative release percentage of Cur was calculated by following equation:
Cumultative release
%
of
Cur =
∑
t = 0
t
M t
M 0
Where Mt is cumulative amount of Cur released of each sample, t is time of release of Cur loaded and M0 is the initial weight of Cur-loaded in nano-carrier.

Primary human fibroblast and Hela cancer cells were purchased from the National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI, Tehran). The cell lines were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco) and passage with 10% (v/v) FBS and penicillin/streptomycin (100 IU/mL and 100 μg/mL, respectively), then we incubated and preserved the cells under 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Then the cells were harvest using a 0.25% trypsin/EDTA solution, as reached to 85%. Each test was performed triplicate.

To cytotoxicity assessment of nano-carrier on fibroblast and Hela cells, the MTT assay was used. The cells were picked up and placed into a 96-well plate (Nunc, Denmark) with concentration of 104 cells/well. Then the plates were treated with different amounts of nano carriers (250, 125, 63, 31, 16, 8, 4, 2 μg/mL) and (2500, 1250, 625, 313, 156, 78, 39, μg/mL). 24 and 48 h after treatment and added the same solution of (3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide) to each well. Culture media and 5% triton X-100 were used as negative and positive control, respectively and the plates were incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. Then the supernatants removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well and incubated for 20 min and the absorbance was measured by an ELISA plate reader at λ = 570 nm. The percentage of viability and cytotoxicity was achieved using the following relation [45]:
Viability
%
=
mean absorbance of toxicant − OD
blank
mean absorbance of negative control − OD
blank
× 100
Cytotoxicity
%
=
1 −
mean absorbance of toxicant mean absorbance of negative control
× 100

To study the US influence on drug release a multiwave ultrasonic homogenizer (Sonicator 3200; Development of Ultrasonic Technology Co.; Tehran, Iran) was used. The phase analyses of each sample were performed by a XRD (Philips X' pert Pro MPD, Amsterdam, The Netherlands) equipped with a graphite-filtered Cu Kα (k = 0.154 nm) radiation in the 2θ range from 10 to 90 with a step size of 0.021 and a measuring time of 0.8 s per point. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) of Tescan MIRA3 FEG- SEM (Prague, Czech Republic) was used to evaluate sample morphology. A FT-IR spectrophotometer model Nicolet Magna IR 550 spectrometer (New York, USA) was used to measure the FT-IR spectra of samples. Magnetic measurements were carried out at room temperature using a PMC MicroMag 3900 model vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) equipped with a 1 Tesla magnet (Magnetic Daghigh Daneshpajouh Co.; Kashan, Iran). Drug loading and release of Cur were performed by ultraviolet-visible array spectroscopy (UV–Vis, Tief Sanj Pishro Pajohesh Co (Laboratory Industial and Research System Co.); Tehran, Iran). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Rigaku TG 8120 instrument within the temperature range from 0 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in the air (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Zeta Potential Analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., London, UK). To measure the BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) specific surface areas and porosity of samples the adsorption isotherm of nitrogen adsorption in −196 °C was measured by a gas adsorption apparatus (Towse-e Hesgarsazan-e Asia (Sensiran) Co. Tehran, Iran).

The XRD patterns of ZnFe2O4, mZnO NPs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nano-carriersis shown in Fig. 1 . The peaks at 2θ values of 30.1, 35.3, 37.1, 43.0, 53.5, 56.3, 62.4, 70.6 and 74.5° could be attributed to (220, 311, 222, 400, 422, 511, 440, 533, 622) planes of the cubic ZnFe2O4 with spinel structure (JCPDS No. 22–1012), respectively [46]. Therefore, no peaks from other phases were detected, suggesting the high purity of the as-synthesized ZnFe2O4 nano-crystals. The patterns at 2theta values of 30.21, 34.15, 36.87, 47.13, 55.98, 65.78, 74.18 and 75.94 degrees attributed to the (100,002,101,102,110,103,112,202) planes of hexagonal wurzite nano-structure of ZnO, respectively (JCPDS 36–1451) [47]. The special diffraction pattern peaks at 2θ = 26 belonging to the separate N-GQDs are shown in the N-GQDs [48].

The morphology and particle size of the as-prepared N-GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs was presented by FESEM in Fig. 2a, b and c, respectively. FESEM images (Fig. 2(a)) show GQDs with uniform spherical shape and an average size of 5 ± 1 nm. Fig. 2(a) and (c) shows the FESEM image confirming the uniform spherical shape and an average size of 40 ± 15 and 25 ± 10 nm in diameter, respectively.

Also to assess of chemical composition the EDX analyses were used and clearly confirmed the presence of Zn, Fe, Zn, C, N and O elements (Fig. 2d), thus confirming the successful synthesis of the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs nanocarriers.

The crystalline and morphology structure of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs nano-carriers are presented by the TEM images in Fig. 2(e). As shown in Fig. 2(e), ZnFe2O4 exists in a cubic morphology with diameter approximately below 75 nm. The average diameter of N-GQDs is 3–10 nm. The SAEDP image of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs nano-carriers shows ring patterns which is the characteristic feature of polycrystalline nanomaterials (Fig. 2(f)). The appearance of three sets of diffraction rings further confirms the coexistence of N-GQDs, mZnO and ZnFe2O4.

The FT-IR spectra of (a) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs-Cur, (b) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs, (c) ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs-Cur and (d) Cur were placed in Fig. 3 . As shown in this figure, the absorption band at 2880 cm−1, 1105 cm−1, 1725 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 related to the aromatic CH, C-O-C, CO, and C-OH groups stretching peak of N-GQD, GQD and Cur [49–51]. The peaks in the area of 2600–3300 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH bending vibrations of N-GQDs and Cur [52,53]. ZnFe2O4 NPs show the characteristic FeO peak at 565.54 cm−1 [54]. Absorption peaks in the range 400–4000 cm−1 indicated the existence of nano-carriers and nano-carriers-Cur. The absorption peaks corresponding to the functional groups of Cur were observed at much lower intensities such as 1629 cm−1 (aromatic moiety CC stretching vibration), and 1509 cm−1 (-C-O- and -C=C- vibration band) indicating that the main Cur peaks were retained when it was incorporated in the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs, and these results suggested that it was incorporated into the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs [55].

It is well established that manipulating magnetic nano-carriers is an important factor for carrying loaded drugs to targeted situations and also controlling drug delivery by an external magnetic field. Fig. 4 shows the magnetic properties of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs nano-carriers which were obtained by VSM. Both samples have a typical hysteresis loop, that proved a hard magnetic behavior for both nano-materials [56]. Also the results show that the saturation magnetization (Ms) and remanent magnetization (Mr) values increased upon the addition of N-GQDs as compared to GQDs. The coercivity (Hc) of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs were higher than that obtained for ZnFe2O4@mZnO@GQDs. One of the most important factors for biomedical applications of nan-carriers is the magnetic properties at room temperature. Table 1 represented the coercivity, remanence, and saturation magnetization of two samples.

To study the thermal stability of N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs ternary nanocomposite, TGA–DTA were investigated under an N2 atmosphere. TGA–DTA curves are shown in Fig. 5 , in which different mass loss steps are displayed. N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs ternary nanocomposite showed two and five mass loss steps, respectively. The bare N-GQDs had a weight loss of 54% when the temperature was changed from 30 °C to 800 °C, because of loss of absorbed water and demolition of the hydroxide groups. The weight loss of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs was 23.1% from 40 °C to 800 °C, because of the evaporation of adsorbed water and demolition of organic groups. By comparison to weight loss of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and N-GQDs, it is concluded that the grafting amount of N-GQDs to be 30.9%.

DLS analysis was exploited to find out the zeta potential (ZP) and hydrodynamic diameter of the prepared ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs nano-carriers and the corresponding results are listed in (Fig. 6a–f). As illustrated in Fig. 6e and f, the determined hydrodynamic size of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO is in the range of 18 and 37 nm, respectively. The negative and positive charge at pH 7.4 and 5.5, respectively, is resultant of a number of amine‑oxygen and oxygen-containing functional groups at both N-GQDs and GQDs. The negative and positive zeta potential of ZnFe2O4@N-GQDs nano-carriers was reported higher than of ZnFe2O4@GQDs, it can be related to the protonation or deprotonation of amine-groups moieties on the surface of ZnFe2O4@N-GQDs. The positive zeta potential of nano-carriers was reported at pH 5.5, it can be related to the protonation of (or hydronation) amine‑oxygen groups and oxygen moieties on the surface of N-GQDs and GQDs, respectively [57]. Thus, the zeta potential analysis shows that electrical charge on surface of N-GQDs and GQDs with cancer cell membranes can lead to strong attractive force among the nano-carriers with cell membrane [58]. So, the Cur release was increased at pH 5.5 (shown in Scheme 3 ).

BET specific surface area (SBET), BJH pore volume (Pvol), and pore size (Psize) of the synthesized ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs nano-carrier were measured and results were listed in Table 2 . The ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs nano-carrier displays type IV isotherm with a discrete hysteresis loop (H3 type) in the high relative pressure range, demonstrating the presence of a mesoporous structure of ZnO [59] (See Fig. 7.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-cancer activity and therapeutic efficacy study on the HeLa cell line
ce:section.ce:para: The ISO 10993-5 standard classifies cytotoxics as: non-toxic for cell viability above 80%; Poor toxicity for cell viability between 80 and 60%; moderate cytotoxicity for cell viability between 60%–40% and finally strong cytotoxicity for cell viability below 40% [60]. The safety and toxicity are very important factors in the design of nano-carriers in biomedical usage. To assessment of biocompatibility of nanocarriers, in vitro cytotoxicity of ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- QDs -Cur, ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs -Cur, and Cur on the HeLa cells line were studied using MTT assay. ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs nano-carrier exhibited excellent biocompatibility and cell viability up to 77 μg/mL for 24 h (94%) and 48 h (78%) (Fig. 8a and b). As shown in Fig. 8a and b, HeLa cells exhibited lower viability with treatment of Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO nano-carrierscompared with that of free Cur for all the tested concentrations, indicating the largely increased therapeutic efficiency to cancer cells with assembly Cur nanocarriers. The results indicated that the nano-carrier almost has cytotoxicity on HeLa cells compared to free Cur. Addition of Cur enhances the cytotoxicity of nano-carrier. Therefore, considering the magnetic behavior and acceptable dispersibility in aqueous solution, these nano-carriers are engaged candidates for controlled targeted Cur nano-carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity study of nano-carriers against normal human fibroblast cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of different concentrations of ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- GQDs, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- QDs -Cur, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs -Cur, and Cur (2–250 μg/mL) was studied against normal human fibroblast cells for 24 and 48 h after treatment, as shown in Fig. 9(a and b). A biocompatibility study revealed suitable biocompatibility of the ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N/RGO nano-carriersagainst normal human fibroblast cells. These nanocarriers were found to be highly biocompatible, ie, about 60 and 65% cell viability against normal human fibroblast cell lines was found for ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs at 125 μg/ml concentration in after 24 and 48 h treatment respectively. Moreover, for a Cur concentration of 63 μg/mL, the cell viability with Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs (60 and 58% at 24 and 48 h) was even higher than for Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO (45 and 20% at 24 and 48 h), which could contribute to the improved targeting efficiency to cancer cells (HeLa) of N-GQDs modified ZnFe2O4@mZnO nano-carrier surface. In addition, ZnFe2O4@mZnO- N-GQDs nano-carrierswere found to be highly biocompatible with normal human fibroblast cell with various concentrations from 2 μg/mL to 63 μg/mL for 24 and 48 h and cell viability was found to be 100%. Our study confirmed that addition of N-GQDs to ZnFe2O4@mZnO decreases its toxicity but addition of Cur increases its toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and controlled releaseing of nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para:
Nano-carriers were measured in two different pH treatments: control (pH 7.4) and acidic (pH 5.5). Fig. 10(a and b) show the kinetic release curves of nano-carriers under these two conditions. These plots were obtained based on the sustained and slowed release by using the absorption of the peak at 420 nm. Fig. 10(a and b) shows the cumulative releasing profiles of Cur from the nano-carriers obtained (a) ultrasound and pH dually responsive and (b) single pH-responsive at pHs of 5.5 and 7.4. At pH = 7.4 the rate of release was very slow. The release percentages of Cur from the nano-carriers of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs are listed in Tables 4 and 5
.

The design of intelligent releasing nano-carrier systems containing N-GQDs by pH is so important in controlling anti-cancer drug delivery because the pH of tumors is acidic in comparison to normal tissues [66]. At pH 5.5, the electrostatic attraction makes Cur easy to release from nanocarriers. So, the pH-sensitive release of Cur-loaded nano-carriersis very useful in targeting the low-pH intracellular of tumors, increasing the cytotoxicity for cancer cells and minimal effect on the normal cells because of lowest drug releasing from the nano-carriers under normal physiological conditions [67]. There is weak hydrogen bonding between N-GQDs and Cur, so it could achieve a good slow-release effect [68].

The Chemo-sonodynamic therapy is another new strategy for cancer treatment, because the low-intensity ultrasound caused the activation of Cur loaded magnetic nanosonosensitizers (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs) [69]. With synthesized ultrasound sensitive nanocarriers, chemo-sonodynamic therapy could be considered as: 1) a generator of cellular reactive oxygen species and mitochondrial damage, and 2) inducer effect through the release of Cur loaded magnetic nano-sonosensitizers [70]. It will be interesting if the magnetic ZnFe2O4@mZnO NPs are used as a nanosonosensitizing agent in Chemo-sonodynamic therapy. These magnetic nanosonosensitizer could be inter in the body of patients via intravenously or intra-arterially. By using a strong inhomogeneous magnetic field in the tumor area, these nano-carriers will be collected at tumor [71]. The magnetic ZnFe2O4@mZnO NPs increase the attenuation of the ultrasonic wave within the tumor area and so increases the generation of cellular reactive oxygen species and Cur by the chemo-sonodynamic therapy [72]. While the normal tissue will be protected. The result shows that the as-prepared ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs core-interlayer-shell nanostructures were suitable for localized chemo-sonodynamic therapy in US controlled and low pH (5.5) anti-cancer drug prolonged and sustained release and destroy of cancer cells in tumor sites.

In summary, a novel biocompatible nano-carrier (ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs) for controlled release of anti-cancer drug and pH/US-triggered smart drug release, was firstly constructed on the basis of magnetic nanosonosensitizer as the US absorbance agent, and N-GQDs were then coupled to improve the biocompatibility and hydrophilicity, respectively. This pH/ US-triggered controlled and sustained release of Cur from nano-carriersmay be beneficial to decrease the side effects to normal tissues and increase tumor selectivity toward tumor tissues. Herein, N-GQDs based core-interlayer-shell structure hybrid nanocomposites can also be used for drug delivery the strong π-π interaction allows for the adsorption of various aromatic drugs such as Cur. The anti-cancer Cur drug loading efficiency in ZnFe2O4@mZnO-N-GQDs was higher than that of ZnFe2O4@mZnO-GQDs. Notably, these results indicated that the low pH and chemo-sonodynamic therapy NPs are a desirable unique carrier drug delivery systems for cancer treatment.

Ms. Media Hafezi, Mojtaba Rostami and Dr. Mahdi Fasihi-Ramandi: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft; Dr. Asieh Hosseini and Prof. Alireza Badiei‎: Writing- Review & Editing; Prof. Mehdi Rahimi-Nasrabadi and Prof. Farhad Ahmadi: Funding acquisition, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thanks from National Institute for Medical Research Development (NIMAD) for supporting of this work with grant number of 983267.
